stimulus:
Text 1

Dutch painters of the early 1600s seldom included tools of their craft in self-portraits. Painting had not yet attained high social status, so most painters chose to emphasize their education or fine clothing instead. In the city of Haarlem, the first painter to depict himself with a palette was Frans Hals. A portrait of his from about 1640 shows Hals holding a brush in mid-stroke.

 

Text 2

Among Haarlem painters, references to the act of painting were uncommon in self-portraits until the mid-seventeenth century. However, Judith Leyster's Self-Portrait, completed around 1633, stands out as an earlier example. In the painting, Leyster turns toward the viewer with a brush in one hand and a palette in the other, while a half-finished canvas rests on her easel.
stem: Based on the texts, how would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?

answerOptions.content: Leyster's Self-Portrait was painted before Hals's portrait and also refers to the artist's craft.
Hals and Leyster were among the most successful painters in Haarlem at the time.
Leyster was likely inspired by Hals's depiction of a brush and palette.
Leyster's portrait was meant to emphasize her social position rather than her work.

rationale:
Choice A is correct. Text 2 places Leyster's brush-and-palette self-portrait around 1633, earlier than Hals's circa-1640 portrait, directly contradicting Text 1's claim that Hals was first.

Choices B, C, and D introduce ideas not supported by Text 2.
